nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving or still forest streams, pools, and swampy areas with dense leaf litter and overhanging vegetation; often found in heavily shaded coastal lowland habitats near Cape Lopez (Port-Gentil), Gabon

decorPreferences: dense floating plants (Amazon frogbit, water lettuce) to dim lighting
leaf litter (Indian almond leaves, oak leaves) for tannin release and spawning substrate
fine-leaved plants or Java moss for egg deposition
driftwood and twisted roots to break line of sight
dark substrate (black sand or fine dark gravel) to enhance coloration

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild feeding on small invertebrates, insect larvae, and tiny crustaceans. In captivity readily accepts high-quality micro-pellets, small flake food, frozen or freeze-dried bloodworms, daphnia, brine shrimp, and white mosquito larvae. Live foods eagerly taken and useful for conditioning breeders. Unlike many killifish, the Chocolate Australe reliably accepts prepared dry foods — a key reason it is regarded as the most beginner-friendly killifish. Feed small amounts twice daily; avoid overfeeding as the species is susceptible to internal fat deposits.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (ember tetras, neon tetras, cardinal tetras)
dwarf gourami (with caution in smaller tanks)
freshwater shrimp in species tanks or species-focused setups (adults generally ignore adult shrimp)
avoidWith: aggressive or boisterous fish that will outcompete at feeding
fast-moving fish that stress timid males
other male Aphyosemion australe in small tanks without ample cover (conspecific male rivalry)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Aphyosemion australe — shows wild-type orange-red coloration; chocolate morph is selectively bred brown/amber variant with same finnage
Male Aphyosemion australe showing lyre-tail extension; may depict wild-type coloration rather than chocolate morph specifically

summary: Selectively bred brown-toned color morph of Aphyosemion australe, a non-annual West African killifish from coastal Gabon. Males display lyre-shaped caudal fins with iridescent spotting. The most beginner-friendly killifish — non-annual, long-lived, and accepts prepared dry foods readily. Soft slightly acidic water preferred; tolerates moderate hardness. Tight lid essential — confirmed jumper.
careNotes: The Chocolate Australe is the selectively bred chocolate (brown/amber) morph of Aphyosemion australe, the Cape Lopez Lyretail — the wild type displays vivid orange-red body coloration, while the chocolate form exhibits warm brown to amber-chocolate tones, both with the same iridescent blue-green spots and elongated lyre-shaped caudal fin extensions in males. Females are smaller and more plainly colored. Native to shallow, heavily shaded forest streams and coastal lowland pools near Cape Lopez (present-day Port-Gentil), Gabon, and adjacent parts of Cameroon and Congo, this species inhabits soft, acidic, tannin-stained water. Ideal parameters are pH 6.0–7.0, gH 2–8, and temperature 22–24°C, though the species tolerates up to pH 7.5 and moderate hardness (gH to 12) better than most killifish — a key reason it is widely recommended as the entry-point killifish. Unlike annual killifish (Nothobranchius, many Austrolebias), A. australe is non-annual: it does not require a dry-season diapause and eggs hatch normally in water without desiccation. Captive lifespan of 3–5 years is achievable under good conditions, substantially longer than annual species. A 40 L (minimum) species tank or a quiet community aquarium is suitable; a pair or a trio (1 male : 2 females) is recommended. Males display intermittent intraspecific rivalry — provide ample sight breaks via plants or driftwood. Floating plants and a dim photoperiod bring out the best coloration. A tight-fitting lid is non-negotiable: A. australe is an accomplished jumper. Water changes of 20–30% weekly maintain the soft, clean conditions the species thrives in; avoid large sudden changes in pH or hardness. The chocolate form breeds true and is widely available in the hobby.
breedingNotes: Aphyosemion australe is a plant spawner (not a peat diver) — eggs are deposited individually on fine-leaved plants, Java moss, or spawning mops. A pair can be conditioned with live and frozen foods; spawning occurs over several days. Eggs take approximately 14–21 days to hatch at 22–24°C in a shallow dish or floating container with some water from the main tank. Alternatively, eggs can be collected by removing the mop and gently squeezing it over a small tray — visually inspect for white eggs and discard. Fry are small but free-swimming at hatch and will accept micro-worms, vinegar eels, baby brine shrimp, and finely crushed micro-pellets from day one. Separate fry from adults to prevent predation. Breeding difficulty is considered easy to moderate — the species is approachable for beginners willing to manage eggs. The chocolate form breeds true; avoid crossing with wild-type orange-red fish if maintaining the color strain.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Aphyosemion australe
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~6 cm TL for males), temperature range (20–26°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (36–215 µS/cm, soft), native range (coastal Gabon, Cameroon, Congo), habitat (shallow forest streams and pools), diet (invertebrates; accepts dry foods in captivity), breeding method (plant spawner, eggs hatch in water 14–21 days), non-annual status, and compatibility notes. Identified as the most beginner-accessible killifish species.

additional.name: FishBase — Aphyosemion australe
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (males to 6.0 cm TL), temperature (20–26°C), taxonomy (Nothobranchiidae, Cyprinodontiformes), native range (coastal Gabon/Cameroon/Congo, Cape Lopez type locality), diet type (invertivore/carnivore), and habitat (demersal in shallow freshwater). FishBase records wild-type species; chocolate form is a captive selectively-bred color morph.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Killifish Care Guide
additional.notes: Confirms Aphyosemion australe as the recommended starter killifish — non-annual, prepared-food accepting, soft acidic water, ~6 cm, 40+ L tank. Jump risk and tight lid requirement explicitly noted. Temperature 72–80°F (22–27°C) and pH 6.0–7.5 cited. Chocolate form acknowledged as common captive morph.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Aphyosemion australe profile
additional.notes: Confirms non-annual status, West African coastal range, lyre-tail males, plant-spawning breeding method, soft slightly acid water preference, and acceptance of frozen and prepared foods. Lifespan noted as 3–5 years in captivity under good conditions. Chocolate and wild-type (orange) forms discussed as common hobby variants.

additional.name: FishBase — Aphyosemion australe taxonomy
additional.notes: Taxonomic placement confirmed: Order Cyprinodontiformes, Family Nothobranchiidae (Aphyosemion was historically placed in Aplocheilidae; molecular work re-placed African killifish clade in Nothobranchiidae). Used for taxonomy.family and taxonomy.order fields.

fish.breedingNotes: Plant spawner; eggs deposited on Java moss or spawning mops. Eggs hatch in 14–21 days at 22–24°C in water — no desiccation diapause required (non-annual). Fry accept baby brine shrimp and micro-worms from hatch. Condition breeders with live/frozen foods. Chocolate form breeds true.
